# Part 2 — Tinkercad Simulation {.sdaia-dark background-gradient="linear-gradient(135deg, #1C355E, #00C9A7)"}

## Why we simulate before we build

Wiring a motor driver wrong can short-circuit the battery or blow up components. We will use **Tinkercad** to wire our first virtual motor driver. If we make a mistake, we only blow up pixels!

::: {.callout-tip}
## Your Task
Go to `tinkercad.com/circuits` and start a new project.
:::

## Wiring the L298N Motor Driver

:::::: {.columns}

::::: {.column width="50%"}

We need to connect 3 types of wires:

1. **Power In**: Battery `+` to `12V`, Battery `-` to `GND`.
2. **Motor Out**: Motor wires to `OUT1` and `OUT2`.
3. **Logic In**: Arduino Pins to `IN1` and `IN2`.

*And crucially, Arduino `GND` must connect to the Battery `GND` so they share the same 0V reference!*

:::::

::::: {.column width="50%"}

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import schemdraw
import schemdraw.elements as elm

with schemdraw.Drawing() as d:
    # Set spacing to wide
    d.config(unit=1.5)
    # IC pins are ordered so they draw properly (Left/Right draw Bottom->Top)
    # Increase edgepadH significantly to move bottom side pins (GND, OUT2) up
    l298 = elm.Ic(pins=[
        elm.IcPin(name='GND', side='left'),   # Left Bottom
        elm.IcPin(name='12V', side='left'),   # Left Top
        elm.IcPin(name='IN1', side='bottom'), # Bottom Left
        elm.IcPin(name='IN2', side='bottom'), # Bottom Right
        elm.IcPin(name='OUT2', side='right'), # Right Bottom
        elm.IcPin(name='OUT1', side='right')  # Right Top
    ], w=5, h=5, pinspacing=1.5, edgepadH=1.5).label('L298N', loc='center')
    d += l298
    
    # Left side: Battery loop
    p12v = getattr(l298, '12V')
    pgnd = getattr(l298, 'GND')
    
    # Battery Loop
    d += elm.Line().at(p12v).left().length(2.5)
    batt = elm.BatteryCell().down().label('12V\nBatt', loc='left')
    d += batt
    d += elm.Line().toy(pgnd)
    d += elm.Line().right().to(pgnd)

    # Bottom side: Logic (Pin 9 / Pin 10)
    d += elm.Line().at(l298.IN1).down().length(1.5).label('Pin 9', loc='bottom', color='blue')
    d += elm.Line().at(l298.IN2).down().length(1.5).label('Pin 10', loc='bottom', color='blue')
    
    # Arduino GND branching from GND pin
    # Position it precisely between the IC and the battery to avoid overlap
    d += elm.Line().at(pgnd).left().length(1.25)
    d += elm.Line().down().length(1.5).label('Arduino\nGND', loc='bottom', color='blue')

    # Right side: Motor
    d += elm.Line().at(l298.OUT1).right().length(1.5)
    motor = elm.Motor().down().label('DC\nMotor', loc='right')
    d += motor
    d += elm.Line().toy(l298.OUT2)
    d += elm.Line().left().to(l298.OUT2)
    
    d.draw()
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Wait, what is 12V vs 5V?
The driver takes the strong battery power (up to 12V) on one side, and the safe 5V logic signals from the Arduino on the other side.
:::

:::::

::::::

## Writing the Tinkercad Code

Once wired, let's write the code to spin the motor. We use the exact same `digitalWrite` commands we learned for LEDs.

```cpp
void setup() {
  pinMode(9, OUTPUT);  // IN1
  pinMode(10, OUTPUT); // IN2
}

void loop() {
  // Spin Forward
  digitalWrite(9, HIGH);
  digitalWrite(10, LOW);
  delay(1000);

  // Stop completely
  digitalWrite(9, LOW);
  digitalWrite(10, LOW);
  delay(1000);
}
```

## Testing the Simulation

Click **"Start Simulation"** in Tinkercad. 

Observe the virtual motor:
1. Does it spin? What RPM is indicated?
2. **Change the Code:** Make `IN1` LOW and `IN2` HIGH. What happens to the spin direction?

::: {.fragment}
We reverse the direction of a DC motor by swapping the `HIGH` and `LOW` signals!
:::
